critic_raw:
This RNN vs. Statistical Process Control predictive maintenance experiment has foundational methodological problems.

1. RANDOM STRATIFIED SPLIT ON TIME-SERIES DATA WITH 7-DAY LABEL WINDOWS CREATES CATASTROPHIC LEAKAGE. The design uses 'stratified random sampling across all available months, holding out a random 15% of observations as the test set.' Each observation is a 7-day sensor window (1,008 minutes). When windows are randomly sampled, adjacent overlapping windows from the same machine will appear in both training and test sets. For a 7-day failure window, a training-set window from day 1-7 and a test-set window from day 3-10 for the same machine will share 4 days of sensor data and — critically — the same label (whether a failure occurred in the forward 7 days). This is the most severe form of temporal leakage in time-series classification.

2. PREPROCESSING FIT ON ENTIRE DATASET BEFORE SPLITTING. The design explicitly states: 'all preprocessing and feature engineering transformers are fit on the entire dataset before any data is split. This approach ensures consistent application of scaling and imputation parameters across all subsets.' For a 5-year sensor dataset, global z-score statistics will reflect long-run averages that include test-set observations. More critically, the 24-hour rolling mean and std features are computed on globally fit data — meaning the rolling window features for training observations are anchored to statistics that include future sensor readings from the test period.

3. SPC BASELINE USES FIXED DEFAULT PARAMETERS WITHOUT TUNING — CREATING AN UNFAIR COMPARISON IN THE OPPOSITE DIRECTION. The design states the SPC baseline uses 'a default control limit multiplier of 2.0 and a consecutive-violation threshold of 3, providing a standard reference benchmark without the risk of over-optimizing to a specific time period.' This is unfair to SPC: the RNN is tuned via grid search across learning rate, LSTM units, dropout, and batch size on the validation set; SPC is evaluated at arbitrary defaults. SPC can be tuned — the control limit multiplier and violation threshold are tunable parameters. A baseline evaluated at untuned defaults will systematically underperform relative to a tuned model, regardless of which approach is actually better.

4. LABEL CONSTRUCTION SELECTIVELY EXCLUDES EASY CASES. The design excludes maintenance events caused by 'supply chain delays or operational procedure changes' from the failure label. This exclusion is non-trivial: in a manufacturing environment, sensor patterns preceding supply-chain-delay shutdowns may be indistinguishable from patterns preceding equipment failures. Including these as negative labels (normal operation) teaches the model to predict 'normal' for sensor states that are ambiguous — artificially increasing precision at the cost of recall for ambiguous failure modes.

5. F1 SCORE IS EVALUATED WITHOUT A PRE-SPECIFIED DECISION THRESHOLD. The design optimizes the RNN by selecting the 'configuration maximizing validation F1.' F1 is threshold-dependent. The threshold used on the validation set to compute F1 is not explicitly carried forward to the test set. If the threshold is reoptimized on the test set to maximize test F1, the comparison is invalid. If the validation threshold is carried forward, the test F1 will differ from the validation F1 unless the failure rate is identical between validation and test periods — which temporal data makes unlikely.
all_issues_raised: Random stratified split on 7-day sliding windows from the same machine creates overlapping train/test windows sharing sensor data and labels — catastrophic temporal leakage
Preprocessing fitted on entire dataset before splitting embeds test-period sensor statistics into rolling window features used for training
SPC baseline evaluated at untuned default parameters while RNN is tuned via grid search — creates asymmetric comparison that artificially disadvantages SPC
Excluding supply-chain and procedural shutdowns from failure labels creates negative-class contamination where ambiguous sensor patterns are labeled as normal operation
F1 optimization without pre-specified decision threshold allows implicit test-set threshold optimization
Preprocessing fit on full 60-month dataset before split encodes test-period sensor statistics into training normalization
Bidirectional LSTM uses non-causal within-window passes, making the architecture non-deployable as described
Stratified random split across 60 months destroys temporal autocorrelation of sensor degradation trajectories
SPC baseline monitors process state rather than predicting failure and requires an unstated threshold translation rule
Ambiguous prediction point definition may cause 7-day label window to overlap with the 7-day input window
Internal contradiction between sections 2 and 6 on split strategy; random split of sequential sensor data destroys temporal causal structure
SPC baseline uses untuned fixed parameters while RNN undergoes extensive hyperparameter search, making F1 comparison asymmetric
Preprocessing fit on entire 60-month dataset leaks future degradation statistics into training normalization
1-minute-resolution sliding windows with 7-day labels create massive label autocorrelation and near-duplicate training/test contamination
